questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Bats in the Orchard
For years, the owners of Red Hill Orchard noticed bats flying above the peach trees at dusk. They knew bats ate insects, but they did not know whether the animals made a measurable difference for the orchard. When the local agricultural extension office offered to help with a study, the owners agreed.
Researchers placed small sound monitors near several rows of trees. The monitors did not record human speech. They captured high-frequency bat calls that helped identify when bats were active. The team also compared insect damage on fruit from areas with frequent bat activity and areas where bat calls were recorded less often.
The results were not dramatic, but they were useful. Rows with more bat activity had slightly lower insect damage during part of the summer. The researchers did not claim that bats could replace all pest management. Weather, moth populations, tree health, and timing of harvest also affected the fruit. However, the study suggested that bats were part of a larger system that helped the orchard.
The owners responded by installing bat houses along the edge of the property and reducing unnecessary nighttime lighting near the trees. They also kept using other responsible pest-control methods. The project changed the way they viewed the orchard. It was not just a place where fruit grew. It was a living system in which even quiet nighttime visitors could play a role.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A study helped orchard owners understand that bats may contribute to insect control as part of a larger system.
choices.B: Researchers proved that bats can replace every form of pest control in orchards.
choices.C: The orchard owners removed all peach trees after studying bat calls.
choices.D: Sound monitors were used to record human conversations in the orchard.

questions.correctAnswerText: A study helped orchard owners understand that bats may contribute to insect control as part of a larger system.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that bat activity was studied and found to be one useful part of orchard pest management.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Bats in the Orchard.”
Why did researchers use sound monitors?

choices.A: To capture high-frequency bat calls and identify bat activity
choices.B: To count every peach harvested in the orchard
choices.C: To scare insects away from the trees
choices.D: To record conversations among orchard workers

questions.correctAnswerText: To capture high-frequency bat calls and identify bat activity
questions.explanation: The monitors recorded bat calls so researchers could identify when bats were active.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Bats in the Orchard.”
Which TWO factors did the passage say could affect fruit besides bat activity?

choices.A: Weather
choices.B: Timing of harvest
choices.C: The color of the orchard sign
choices.D: The number of parking spaces
choices.E: The shape of the sound monitors

questions.correctAnswerText: Weather | Timing of harvest
questions.explanation: The passage says weather, moth populations, tree health, and harvest timing also affected fruit.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Bats in the Orchard.”
What can be inferred from the orchard owners’ response to the study?

choices.A: They wanted to support bats while still using other responsible pest-control methods.
choices.B: They believed bats had no relationship to the orchard.
choices.C: They decided to harvest fruit only at night.
choices.D: They stopped caring about insect damage after the study.

questions.correctAnswerText: They wanted to support bats while still using other responsible pest-control methods.
questions.explanation: The owners installed bat houses and reduced lighting, but they also continued using other pest-control methods.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Bats in the Orchard.”
In the sentence “the study suggested that bats were part of a larger system,” system most nearly means [D1].

D1: connected group of parts working together
single object with no relationship to anything else

questions.correct: D1=connected group of parts working together
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=connected group of parts working together
questions.explanation: In context, “system” refers to the orchard environment and its interacting parts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Bats in the Orchard.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Researchers placed sound monitors near several rows of trees.
tiles.B: Rows with more bat activity had slightly lower insect damage during part of the summer.
tiles.C: The owners installed bat houses along the edge of the property.
tiles.D: The owners replaced all pest management with bats.

targets.T2: Evidence that bat activity may have helped
targets.T3: Evidence that the owners acted on the findings

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Researchers placed sound monitors near several rows of trees.; T2=Rows with more bat activity had slightly lower insect damage during part of the summer.; T3=The owners installed bat houses along the edge of the property.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the requested evidence.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Delivery Verification Procedure
To: Receiving Desk, Stockroom, and Department Leads
From: Regional Operations Coordinator
Subject: Delivery Verification Procedure
Beginning April 7, all deliveries must be verified before merchandise is moved to the sales floor or storage areas. This procedure applies to regular shipments, special orders, and replacement items.
When a delivery arrives, the receiving employee must compare the packing slip with the purchase order. Check the item number, description, quantity, and condition of the boxes. If the packing slip does not match the purchase order, or if boxes are damaged, do not sign the final delivery confirmation until a department lead has reviewed the issue.
Shortages, extra items, or visible damage must be recorded on the Delivery Exception Form. Include the delivery date, carrier name, purchase order number, item description, and a brief explanation. If damage is visible, take a photo before the box is opened.
This procedure is not meant to slow down normal receiving work. It is meant to prevent billing errors, missing merchandise, and disagreements with carriers. A signed delivery confirmation may limit the store’s ability to dispute a problem later. Questions should be directed to a department lead before the driver leaves the receiving area.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how employees should verify deliveries and report problems before accepting them
choices.B: To allow employees to sign delivery confirmations without checking shipments
choices.C: To require customers to inspect boxes at the receiving desk
choices.D: To cancel all special orders and replacement items

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how employees should verify deliveries and report problems before accepting them
questions.explanation: The document explains how to compare documents, inspect shipments, and report exceptions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Delivery Verification Procedure.”
What should an employee compare when a delivery arrives?

choices.A: The packing slip and the purchase order
choices.B: The driver’s lunch schedule and the store hours
choices.C: The sales floor layout and the employee handbook
choices.D: The customer receipt and the parking permit

questions.correctAnswerText: The packing slip and the purchase order
questions.explanation: Employees must compare the packing slip with the purchase order.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Delivery Verification Procedure.”
Which TWO problems must be recorded on the Delivery Exception Form?

choices.A: Shortages
choices.B: Visible damage
choices.C: The driver’s route preference
choices.D: The store’s weekly sales total

questions.correctAnswerText: Shortages | Visible damage
questions.explanation: Shortages, extra items, and visible damage must be recorded.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Delivery Verification Procedure.”
Why should employees avoid signing the final delivery confirmation before a department lead reviews a problem?

choices.A: Signing may limit the store’s ability to dispute the problem later.
choices.B: Signing automatically sends merchandise to the customer’s home.
choices.C: Signing removes the need to compare item numbers.
choices.D: Signing allows damaged boxes to repair themselves.

questions.correctAnswerText: Signing may limit the store’s ability to dispute the problem later.
questions.explanation: The document warns that signing a delivery confirmation may affect later disputes.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Delivery Verification Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning April 7, all deliveries must be verified before merchandise is moved.”
choices.B: “This procedure applies to regular shipments, special orders, and replacement items.”
choices.C: “It is meant to prevent billing errors, missing merchandise, and disagreements with carriers.”
choices.D: “Questions should be directed to a department lead before the driver leaves the receiving area.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “It is meant to prevent billing errors, missing merchandise, and disagreements with carriers.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the reason for the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Use Skills-Based Hiring for Entry-Level Office Jobs
Employers should use skills-based hiring for entry-level office jobs instead of relying too heavily on degree requirements. Many office positions require clear writing, basic spreadsheet use, careful recordkeeping, customer communication, and the ability to learn company systems. A college degree may show persistence, but it does not always prove these specific skills. At the same time, some capable applicants may have gained relevant skills through work, military service, caregiving, community programs, or self-study.
Skills-based hiring does not mean lowering standards. Employers can ask applicants to complete a short writing task, organize sample data, respond to a realistic customer message, or explain how they would prioritize a set of tasks. These exercises can reveal whether a candidate can do the work more directly than a degree requirement can.
This approach can also widen the applicant pool. A company that requires a degree for a job that does not truly need one may screen out strong candidates before reviewing their abilities. Removing unnecessary barriers can help employers find workers who are ready to learn and contribute.
Some managers may worry that skills-based hiring takes more time. That concern is understandable. However, hiring the wrong person also costs time. A practical skills review can help employers make better decisions and give qualified applicants a fairer chance.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should use skills-based hiring for entry-level office jobs when specific abilities matter more than degrees.
choices.B: Employers should stop checking whether applicants can write or organize information.
choices.C: All entry-level office jobs should require advanced degrees.
choices.D: Applicants should be hired without any review of their abilities.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should use skills-based hiring for entry-level office jobs when specific abilities matter more than degrees.
questions.explanation: The author argues that job-related skills may be more relevant than degree requirements for some entry-level office jobs.

choices.B: Some managers worry that hiring takes time.
choices.C: Hiring the wrong person can be costly.
choices.D: A college degree may show persistence.

questions.correctAnswerText: Office jobs may require specific skills such as writing, spreadsheets, recordkeeping, and customer communication.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that the hiring process should test specific abilities.

choices.E: Requiring applicants to provide unrelated personal opinions

questions.correctAnswerText: Asking applicants to complete a short writing task | Asking applicants to organize sample data
questions.explanation: The passage lists both as examples of skills-based hiring.

choices.A: By arguing that hiring the wrong person also costs time
choices.B: By claiming that managers should never interview applicants
choices.C: By saying that all hiring decisions should be random
choices.D: By suggesting that employers ignore customer communication skills

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that hiring the wrong person also costs time
questions.explanation: The author responds to the time concern by noting that poor hiring decisions also take time.

questions.correct: D1=A regional employer found that applicants selected through a writing and spreadsheet exercise stayed in entry-level office roles longer than applicants selected by degree requirement alone.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A regional employer found that applicants selected through a writing and spreadsheet exercise stayed in entry-level office roles longer than applicants selected by degree requirement alone.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by connecting skills-based hiring to better retention.

questions.prompt:

Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some applicants without degrees can demonstrate the skills needed for entry-level office work.
choices.B: Degrees never represent any useful learning.
choices.C: Employers should avoid all hiring standards.
choices.D: Office jobs require no communication or organization.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Seating Chart
Nolan had worked three weddings at Willow House, but always in the background. He folded napkins, carried water pitchers, and learned how to move quickly without seeming rushed. On Saturday morning, the event manager placed a printed seating chart in his hands.
“Table cards go out before the florist arrives,” she said. “Alphabetical by last name. Check twice.”
The ballroom looked peaceful before guests entered. White chairs circled empty tables, and sunlight rested on the polished floor. Nolan spread the cards across a side table. At first, the names looked simple. Then he noticed two guests named Martin Lee, one seated with the bride’s family and one with college friends. Another card had a hyphenated last name that had been alphabetized under the wrong letter.
He felt the room grow warmer. A misplaced card could send a guest to the wrong table and create confusion at the door. Nolan wanted to ask the manager to take the chart back, but she was already outside speaking with the florist.
He took a breath and began again. Last name. First name. Table number. He checked the two Martin Lees against the meal list and corrected the hyphenated name. When the doors opened, guests moved from the entry table to their seats with only the usual small delays.
Later, the manager nodded toward the quiet ballroom. “No one notices a seating chart when it works,” she said.
Nolan smiled. For once, being unnoticed felt like success.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Nolan must handle a precise responsibility that could create confusion if done carelessly.
choices.B: Nolan tries to cancel the wedding before the florist arrives.
choices.C: Nolan argues with two guests about the meal list.
choices.D: Nolan refuses to help because he dislikes the ballroom.

questions.correctAnswerText: Nolan must handle a precise responsibility that could create confusion if done carelessly.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Nolan’s careful handling of the seating chart.

choices.B: The florist has turned off the air conditioning.
choices.C: The manager has asked him to leave the wedding.
choices.D: The guests have already refused to sit down.

questions.correctAnswerText: He realizes mistakes in the seating cards could cause problems for guests.
questions.explanation: Nolan feels pressure because a misplaced card could send a guest to the wrong table.

choices.C: He changes every guest’s table without permission.
choices.D: He stops working as soon as the manager leaves.

questions.correctAnswerText: He creates a careful process to manage the task accurately.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Bank Overdraft Notice
A bank overdraft notice tells an account holder that a transaction was approved or attempted even though the account did not have enough available money. The notice may refer to a debit card purchase, automatic bill payment, check, transfer, or ATM withdrawal. Reading the notice carefully can help the customer understand what happened and what to do next.
One important detail is whether the bank paid the transaction or returned it unpaid. If the bank paid it, the account balance may become negative and an overdraft fee may be charged. If the bank returned it, the customer may still owe a fee to the bank, and the company or person expecting payment may also charge a separate fee.
Another important detail is the available balance at the time of the transaction. This may differ from the balance the customer remembers seeing earlier. Pending purchases, holds, automatic payments, or deposits that have not cleared can affect available money. A customer should not assume that the notice is wrong without reviewing recent account activity.
An overdraft notice is not just a warning about a fee. It is a signal to check the account, contact the bank if something seems incorrect, and adjust future payments if needed. Understanding the notice can help customers avoid repeated overdrafts and protect important payments.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: An overdraft notice helps customers understand a transaction problem, possible fees, and needed account actions.
choices.B: An overdraft notice always means the bank made an error.
choices.C: Customers should ignore overdraft notices if they remember seeing money earlier.
choices.D: A returned payment never creates any fees.

questions.correctAnswerText: An overdraft notice helps customers understand a transaction problem, possible fees, and needed account actions.
questions.explanation: The passage explains what an overdraft notice means and how customers should respond.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Bank Overdraft Notice.”
What may happen if the bank pays a transaction without enough available money?

choices.A: The account balance may become negative and an overdraft fee may be charged.
choices.B: The transaction automatically disappears from the account.
choices.C: The customer can never use the account again.
choices.D: The bank must cancel every future automatic payment.

questions.correctAnswerText: The account balance may become negative and an overdraft fee may be charged.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that a paid overdraft can create a negative balance and a fee.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Bank Overdraft Notice.”
Why might the available balance differ from what the customer remembers seeing earlier?

choices.A: Pending purchases, holds, automatic payments, or uncleared deposits can affect it.
choices.B: Banks never update account balances after transactions.
choices.C: Available balance always includes money from future paychecks.
choices.D: A debit card purchase cannot affect available money.

questions.correctAnswerText: Pending purchases, holds, automatic payments, or uncleared deposits can affect it.
questions.explanation: These factors can change the available balance.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Bank Overdraft Notice.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that customers should review account activity before assuming the notice is wrong?

choices.A: “A bank overdraft notice tells an account holder that a transaction was approved or attempted.”
choices.B: “The notice may refer to a debit card purchase, automatic bill payment, check, transfer, or ATM withdrawal.”
choices.C: “A customer should not assume that the notice is wrong without reviewing recent account activity.”
choices.D: “An overdraft notice is not just a warning about a fee.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A customer should not assume that the notice is wrong without reviewing recent account activity.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports the need to review account activity first.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: It explains what an overdraft notice is, describes key details, and gives advice for responding.
choices.B: It tells a story about one customer opening a new bank account.
choices.C: It compares different banks’ advertisements.
choices.D: It argues that customers should stop using automatic payments.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains what an overdraft notice is, describes key details, and gives advice for responding.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the notice, explains paid versus returned transactions and available balance, and recommends action.

choices.C: The visitor forgot her ID, however the receptionist verified her appointment by phone.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Use Blind Initial Application Review
A company should use blind initial application review for some hiring decisions. In this process, reviewers first see work history, skills, certifications, and responses to job-related questions, but they do not see the applicant’s name, address, graduation year, or other details that may distract from qualifications. The goal is to help reviewers focus on whether the applicant can do the job.
Blind review cannot remove every form of bias, but it can reduce the influence of information that is not related to performance. For example, a reviewer may form assumptions based on a name, neighborhood, or age clue without realizing it. Removing those details during the first review can make the early screening process more consistent.
The process should be used carefully. It works best when job-related questions are clear and when later interview steps still allow candidates to explain their experience fully. Blind review is not a complete hiring system. It is one useful step that can make the first stage fairer and more focused.
Passage B: Improve Hiring Training Instead
A company should not rely on blind initial application review. The idea sounds fair, but it may hide important context that helps reviewers understand an applicant’s path. Gaps in work history, career changes, or nontraditional experience may make more sense when reviewers can see the full application. Removing details may cause reviewers to miss strengths rather than reduce bias.
The company should improve hiring training instead. Reviewers need to learn how to recognize job-related evidence, avoid assumptions, and use consistent scoring guides. A strong rubric can require reviewers to explain why each applicant moves forward. This creates accountability throughout the process, not just during the first screen.
Blind review may also create extra administrative work. Someone must remove details, check documents, and make sure reviewers do not receive incomplete information. If the company wants fairer hiring, it should invest in better reviewer training, clearer job descriptions, and structured interviews rather than hiding parts of applications.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports blind initial review to focus on job-related qualifications, while Passage B argues that reviewer training and structured scoring are better solutions.
choices.B: Passage A argues that companies should hire without applications, while Passage B argues that hiring should stop entirely.
choices.C: Passage A supports hiding job skills, while Passage B supports ignoring all qualifications.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on customer service calls, while Passage B focuses on orchard research.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports blind initial review to focus on job-related qualifications, while Passage B argues that reviewer training and structured scoring are better solutions.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether blind review or improved reviewer practices are better for fair hiring.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about blind initial application review.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: Reviewers may form assumptions based on a name, neighborhood, or age clue without realizing it.
choices.B: Gaps in work history may need context.
choices.C: Training can help reviewers use scoring guides.
choices.D: Someone must remove details from applications.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reviewers may form assumptions based on a name, neighborhood, or age clue without realizing it.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s reason for removing certain details in the first review.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about blind initial application review.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Blind review may hide context that helps explain an applicant’s experience.
choices.B: Strong rubrics can make reviewers explain why applicants move forward.
choices.C: Blind review guarantees that every hiring decision will be unfair.
choices.D: Reviewers should never consider job-related evidence.
choices.E: Names are the only details that matter in hiring.

questions.correctAnswerText: Blind review may hide context that helps explain an applicant’s experience. | Strong rubrics can make reviewers explain why applicants move forward.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims with reasoning about context and accountability.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about blind initial application review.
Which statement from Passage A directly acknowledges a limitation of blind review?

choices.A: “A company should use blind initial application review for some hiring decisions.”
choices.B: “The goal is to help reviewers focus on whether the applicant can do the job.”
choices.C: “Blind review cannot remove every form of bias.”
choices.D: “Removing those details during the first review can make the early screening process more consistent.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Blind review cannot remove every form of bias.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly acknowledges that blind review has limits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about blind initial application review.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors want fairer hiring, but they disagree about the best way to improve the review process.
choices.B: Both authors think job qualifications should be ignored.
choices.C: Both authors believe reviewers never make assumptions.
choices.D: Both authors argue that applications should include no work history.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors want fairer hiring, but they disagree about the best way to improve the review process.
questions.explanation: Both passages discuss fairness in hiring, but they recommend different methods.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about blind initial application review.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data comparing applicant outcomes, reviewer consistency, and hiring quality before and after blind review and reviewer training
choices.B: A list of office decorations in the human resources department
choices.C: A history of the company’s building renovations
choices.D: A survey asking employees what snacks they prefer during interviews

questions.correctAnswerText: Data comparing applicant outcomes, reviewer consistency, and hiring quality before and after blind review and reviewer training
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate whether either method improves hiring fairness and quality.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Printed with a red expiration date, Natalie handed the visitor badges to the trainer.

choices.A: Printed with a red expiration date, Natalie handed the visitor badges to the trainer.
choices.B: Natalie handed the visitor badges printed with a red expiration date to the trainer.
choices.C: Natalie, printed with a red expiration date, handed the visitor badges to the trainer.
choices.D: The trainer handed Natalie printed with a red expiration date.

questions.correctAnswerText: Natalie handed the visitor badges printed with a red expiration date to the trainer.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “visitor badges,” the items printed with a red expiration date.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The manager posted a reminder. The reminder explained the check-in rule.

choices.A: The manager posted a reminder that explained the check-in rule.
choices.B: The reminder posted the manager and explained the check-in rule.
choices.C: Explaining the manager, the check-in rule posted a reminder.
choices.D: The manager posted, and the check-in rule explained the reminder.

questions.correctAnswerText: The manager posted a reminder that explained the check-in rule.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The visitor’s badge was returned before she left the building.
choices.B: The visitors badge was returned before she left the building.
choices.C: The visitors’ badge’s was returned before she left the building.
choices.D: The visitor badges’ was returned before she left the building.

questions.correctAnswerText: The visitor’s badge was returned before she left the building.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the badge belongs to one visitor.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Unit Pricing on Grocery Shelf Labels
Grocery stores should be required to display unit prices clearly on shelf labels. A unit price shows the cost per ounce, pound, quart, sheet, or other standard measure. This information helps shoppers compare products that come in different package sizes. Without unit pricing, a larger package may look like a better deal even when it costs more per unit.
Clear unit pricing is especially helpful when families are trying to manage tight budgets. A shopper comparing cereal boxes, laundry detergent, or canned vegetables should not have to do math in the aisle while other customers wait. Unit prices make comparison faster and fairer. They also help shoppers notice when a smaller sale item is still more expensive than a larger regular-price item.
Stores already update shelf labels for prices, sales, and product codes. Adding or improving unit pricing would be a reasonable consumer-information requirement. The rule could allow exceptions for items where unit comparison does not make sense, such as single prepared foods or products sold individually.
Unit pricing does not tell shoppers which product is best. Quality, dietary needs, storage space, and brand preference still matter. But price per unit gives shoppers a basic fact they can use before deciding. Clear labels support informed choices without telling customers what to buy.
Passage B:
Do Not Add More Shelf Label Requirements
Grocery stores should not face a new legal requirement for unit pricing on every shelf label. The goal of helping shoppers compare prices is reasonable, but the rule may be more complicated than it appears. Products are measured in different ways: ounces, pounds, sheets, loads, servings, or counts. If stores choose the wrong unit, shoppers may be confused rather than helped.
The requirement could also be costly for small stores. Updating label systems, training employees, checking accuracy, and correcting mistakes would take time. Large chains may already have software that calculates unit prices, but smaller independent stores may rely on simpler systems. A mistake on a unit price could lead to customer complaints even when the regular price is correct.
There are other ways to help shoppers. Stores can provide comparison signs in high-volume sections, consumer groups can teach shoppers how to compare package sizes, and store apps or receipts can include more detailed information for customers who want it. A broad rule for every shelf label may not be the best solution.
Stores should provide accurate prices, but lawmakers should avoid adding label requirements that may create confusion and costs without guaranteeing better decisions.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether grocery stores should be required to display unit prices clearly on shelf labels? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
